copy.title: Spurs host Knicks in Finals tomorrow. Monaco and World Cup loom.
copy.sportsAngle:
The Tigers beat the Rays 10-9 in a tight finish, while the Brewers demolished the Giants 16-2 in a one-sided affair yesterday. The Rockies edged the Angels 9-8 in another close contest out west.

Russell Henley won the Charles Schwab Challenge at 13-under, taking the title with a strong final round.

The NBA Finals tip off tomorrow night with New York at San Antonio for Game 1. Monaco Grand Prix is coming up on the Formula 1 calendar. The FIFA World Cup opens June 11—nine days away—and the tournament draw is set.
copy.marketsTake:
The S&P 500 edged up 0.3% while the Nasdaq climbed 0.6%, a modest rally riding on Nvidia's 6.3% surge that kept chip momentum alive. Tesla cratered 4.6% and Apple fell 1.8%, signaling that mega-cap strength is selective and fragile.

The real tension is between AI enthusiasm and everything else. Nvidia's pop is real—the company still controls the compute capacity that powers the training runs everyone's betting on. But Tesla's drop matters because it shows the market isn't buying valuation stories anymore, just execution. Microsoft's 2.3% gain and Apple's decline suggest investors are rotating toward software and services while punishing hardware and energy-intensive manufacturing. Bitcoin's 3.9% drop adds to the anxiety: when crypto slides on a
copy.golfNote: Russell Henley won the Charles Schwab Challenge at 13-under par.
copy.sharpTake:
Detroit walked off Tampa 10-9 yesterday in a game that had no business being that close. The Tigers led 10-3 heading into the eighth inning, then watched the Rays claw back and nearly steal it. This is the kind of win that either means everything or nothing depending on where Detroit's head is in three weeks.

Milwaukee obliterated San Francisco 16-2. That's not a baseball game, that's a crime scene. The Brewers put this away so decisively that the only question is whether anyone in the Giants' front office was taking notes on what competence looks like.

Colorado beat Los Angeles 9-8 in a slugfest. The Rockies are bad and the Angels are worse, but at least someone won. That's baseball in early June when nothing matters except the box score that gets printed.

Russell Henley won the Charles Schwab Challenge at 13-under. The PGA Tour grinds on. No drama, no controversy, just a guy named Russell hitting fairways in Fort Worth.

The Monaco Grand Prix is coming. Formula 1 hits the principality this weekend and the usual suspects will qualify within two tenths of a second of each other

sportsDetail.keyNumber: Tigers won 10–9 in a one-run game that came down to the final at-bat.
sportsDetail.seriesSituation: Detroit takes the win in Tampa. This was a close contest where both offenses showed up but Detroit's lineup had the last word.
sportsDetail.howToWatch: Tigers at Rays · Tuesday, June 2 · Tropicana Field · Final
sportsDetail.groupChatAngle: One-run games in early June are noise. What matters: Detroit's offense is producing in road games. That's a tell for October contention.

marketsDetail.headline: Nvidia surges as chip demand crushes Tesla losses
marketsDetail.secondPara: The Fed's preferred inflation gauge drops Thursday—if it's cooler than expected, rate-cut odds spike and drag down mega-cap tech.
marketsDetail.watchNextWeek: PCE data Thursday, June 5. Determines whether the Fed stays hawkish or signals a cut by September.
marketsDetail.tradeToWatch: NVDA +6.3% on AI server demand staying white-hot, while TSLA -4.6% after missing deliveries and slashing margins—two completely different stories in the same market.
marketsDetail.bringUp: Nvidia's up nearly 180% in the last year. That's not momentum anymore—that's the market pricing in a decade of AI spending that actually has to happen.

golfDetail.whyItMatters: Henley's wire-to-wire at Colonial positions him as a legitimate major threat heading into the U.S. Open in two weeks, and this is his second win in three weeks—he's playing the best golf of his career right now.
golfDetail.recap: Henley shot -13 to win by one over Eric Cole, who finished at -12. Ben Griffin, Alex Smalley, and Mac Meissner all tied for third at -11, meaning three guys within two shots couldn't catch him down the stretch on a course that usually rewards aggression.
golfDetail.bringUp: Colonial's greens this week were firm enough that second shots were rolling out 15-20 yards past the pin—Henley's ball-striking precision meant he was leaving himself manageable lag putts while others were scrambling from the rough.
golfDetail.groupChatAngle: Cole had it won going into Sunday and somehow shot himself right out of it. That's the tell on whether a guy's mentally built for a major.

culture.head: A.J. Brown goes silent as Eagles trade talk heats up
culture.body: The star receiver has disappeared from Twitter as Philadelphia weighs moving him before the draft, a signal that either negotiations are active or he's drawing a line. Brown is owed massive money and has leverage, which usually means radio silence beats the noise. The Eagles need to decide if they're paying him or starting over at receiver. When your best player stops posting, listen.

culture.head: Paraguay's Vallejo fined $65K at French Open for sexist umpire comment
culture.body: The ATP handed out real money for a real problem during the clay court run, setting a precedent that locker room talk doesn't fly on court anymore. Vallejo said something he shouldn't have and paid for it in six figures, which gets attention. Tennis is cleaning up faster than the other tours because it has to. The sport's looking at itself.

culture.head: Inside Out 2 hits theaters this weekend
culture.body: Pixar returns to Riley's head with a sequel that digs into teenage emotion and identity, directed by Kelsey Mann. It's the kind of film that works for kids and lands harder for adults watching their own kids grow up. Worth seeing in a theater that still has them.

numbersContext.context: Detroit walked into Tampa and won 9-10 in what looks like a pitching duel that went sideways fast. Neither team could string together consistent at-bats, which means tomorrow's Game 2 will tell you if this is a series or a fluke.
SPY gained 0.3% today as the market treads water before the Fed's next move. Small moves like this mean the tape is waiting for real news, not pricing in a directional bet yet.
Russell Henley shot -13 at the Charles Schwab Challenge and took command of the field. That score puts him in rare air for 72 holes on the PGA Tour, and if he holds it he wins outright.

sportsAdditional: Milwaukee took apart San Francisco 16–2 on the road, a full-scale dismantling that looked less like a baseball game and more like a batting practice session gone wrong. The Brewers are swinging it right now.
Colorado walked off on Los Angeles 9–8 in a tight one that came down to the final moments. The Rockies found a way in a game that could have gone either direction.
